title: 6 Hidar — St. Ezekiel the Prophet & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "6 Hidar — St. Ezekiel the Prophet & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"6 Hidar — St. Ezekiel the Prophet & The Assembly

narrative\": \"Hidar

St. Ezekiel the Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Nicholas of Myra (Translation of Relics)

St. Anatolius of Constantinople, Archbishop

The sixth day of Hidar brings the monthly Ezekiel into the apostolic month, and the prophet of the valley of dry bones is particularly apt for the month in which the interior life finds its communal purpose. In the apostolic vesting, Ezekiel's great vision is not merely individual spiritual renewal — it is the raising of a great army. The bones that assembled and rose and stood in Ezekiel's vision were not a collection of individuals who happened to be in the same valley; they were an army — the whole house of Israel — organized, equipped, and directed for a purpose that exceeded any single member's capacity. Hidar is the month in which the dry-bone renewals of the previous months are organized into the army's formation, in which the Spirit's individual breathings are gathered into the communal mission that requires all of them together. Ezekiel in Hidar is Ezekiel after the breath — standing before the vast assembled host, asking: what now are you for?

The Translation of the Relics of St. Nicholas of Myra — their movement from Myra in Lycia to Bari in southern Italy in 1087 A.D. — is commemorated on this sixth day as one of the great events in the history of saintly intercession and civic piety. Nicholas, the Archbishop of Myra who fed the poor, dowried the unmarried daughters of bankrupt fathers, freed the unjustly condemned, and became the most widely venerated saint in the Western and Eastern Church alike, continues to exercise his ministry through the translation of his relics: where the saints rest, grace flows, and the movement of his sacred remains from one place to another was itself an apostolic act — the bringing of the intercessory presence to a new location, the vesting of a new community with the saint's particular gift. In Hidar's apostolic month, Nicholas's translation is the image of what the apostolic commission does: it carries the source of grace to where it has not yet arrived.

St. Anatolius of Constantinople was the fifth-century Archbishop who presided over the Council of Chalcedon and who navigated the extraordinarily complex theological and political currents of his era with a combination of theological precision and pastoral flexibility that the tradition honors even where it disputes some of his specific decisions. He was the bishop who had to hold together a Church whose unity was being stressed from multiple directions simultaneously — imperial pressure, theological controversy, regional ecclesiastical rivalries — and who managed to keep the institution functioning as a community of faith through conditions that would have dissolved a less skillful leadership. In the apostolic month of Hidar, Anatolius is the bishop-as-vested-administrator: the one who understands that the apostolic commission includes the unglamorous work of institutional maintenance.

Ezekiel, Nicholas, and Anatolius together on the sixth day present the apostolic vesting in its prophetic, intercessory, and administrative dimensions. Ezekiel sees the army before it forms and announces the Spirit's intention. Nicholas carries the grace across the sea to where it is needed. Anatolius holds the institution together so that the mission has a home to go out from and return to. All three are necessary, and the community that is being vested in Hidar for its apostolic work needs the prophet's vision, the saint's intercession, and the bishop's institutional faithfulness in equal measure.
